item.title: “And, Carpenter, where are these unwholesome thoughts abandoned without remainder? Their abandoning has also been taught. Here, Carpenter, a monk, secluded from sensual desires, secluded…
item.description:
“And, Carpenter, where are these unwholesome thoughts abandoned without remainder? Their abandoning has also been taught. Here, Carpenter, a monk, secluded from sensual desires, secluded from unwholesome states, enters and dwells in the first jhāna, accompanied by initial and sustained thought, with rapture and pleasure born of seclusion. Here, those unwholesome thoughts are abandoned without remainder.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 78 – Samaṇamuṇḍikasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monk, when a person dwells with sense faculties unguarded, without moderation in eating, not committed to wakefulness, not watching over wholesome states, not devoted to…
item.description:
“Monk, when a person dwells with sense faculties unguarded, without moderation in eating, not committed to wakefulness, not watching over wholesome states, not devoted to developing the factors of enlightenment at the beginning and end of the night — then his body feels intoxicated, his eye does not see directions clearly, the Dhamma is not remembered by him. Sloth and torpor overcome his mind and remain. Without gladness, he lives the holy life. He has doubts about the true Dhamma. Therefore, monk, you should train thus: ‘I will guard the sense faculties, have moderation in eating, be committed to wakefulness, watch over wholesome states, and be devoted to developing the factors of enlightenment at the beginning and end of the night.’”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.56
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, the perception of foulness, when developed and cultivated, is of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, having the deathless as its end.…
item.description:
“Monks, the perception of foulness, when developed and cultivated, is of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, having the deathless as its end. For what reason is this said? When a monk dwells much with the perception of foulness, his mind retreats, turns back, and draws away from sexual intercourse, and thus his mind is not strained, becomes peaceful, and disenchantment arises. Just as, monks, a cock’s feather or a sinew thrown into fire retreats, turns back, draws away, and is not strained — so too, when a monk dwells much with the perception of foulness, his mind retreats, turns back, draws away from sexual intercourse, and thus his mind is not strained, becomes peaceful, and disenchantment arises. If a monk dwells much with the perception of foulness, yet his mind still runs after, gets stuck in sexual intercourse, and disenchantment does not arise, then he should understand: ‘The perception of foulness is not developed in me; there is no distinction before and after in me; I have not attained the fruit of development.’ There he is clearly aware. If a monk dwells much with the perception of foulness, his mind retreats, turns back, draws away from sexual intercourse, his mind is not strained, becomes peaceful, and disenchantment arises, then he should understand: ‘The perception of foulness is developed in me; there is a distinction before and after in me; I have attained the fruit of development.’ There he is clearly aware. That the perception of foulness, when developed and cultivated, is of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, having the deathless as its end — for this reason it is said.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 7.49
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: - What is called the wrong path? What destroys day and night? What defiles the holy life? What is a bath without water? - Greed…
item.description:
- What is called the wrong path?
What destroys day and night?
What defiles the holy life?
What is a bath without water?
- Greed is called the wrong path,
Aging destroys day and night,
Women defile the holy life,
Ensnaring humankind —
Austerities and the holy life
Are a bath without water.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 1.58
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And what, monks, is the origination of the world? Dependent on the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The meeting of the three is contact. With…
item.description:
“And what, monks, is the origination of the world? Dependent on the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The meeting of the three is contact. With contact as condition, feeling. With feeling as condition, craving. With craving as condition, clinging. With clinging as condition, existence. With existence as condition, birth. With birth as condition, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise. This is the origination of the world. Dependent on the ear … on the nose … on the tongue … on the body … Dependent on the mind and phenomena, mind-consciousness arises. The meeting of the three is contact. With contact as condition, feeling. With feeling as condition, craving. With craving as condition, clinging. With clinging as condition, existence. With existence as condition, birth. With birth as condition, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise. This, monks, is the origination of the world.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.107
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “‘The world is deficient, longing, a slave to craving’ — that, great king, is the fourth exposition of the Dhamma taught by the Blessed One,…
item.description:
“‘The world is deficient, longing, a slave to craving’ — that, great king, is the fourth exposition of the Dhamma taught by the Blessed One, the Knower, the Seer, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One. Having known, seen, and heard that exposition, I went forth from the household life into homelessness.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 82 – Raṭṭhapālasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And what, monks, is the cessation of suffering? Dependent on the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The meeting of the three is contact. With contact…
item.description:
“And what, monks, is the cessation of suffering? Dependent on the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The meeting of the three is contact. With contact as condition, feeling. With feeling as condition, craving. But with the remainderless fading away and cessation of that same craving, clinging ceases. With the cessation of clinging, existence ceases. With the cessation of existence, birth ceases. With the cessation of birth, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair cease. Such is the cessation of this whole mass of suffering. This is the cessation of suffering. Dependent on the ear … on the nose … on the tongue … on the body … Dependent on the mind and phenomena, mind-consciousness arises … With the cessation of craving, clinging ceases … Such is the cessation of this whole mass of suffering. This is the cessation of suffering.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.106
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, these five things, when developed and cultivated, lead to the destruction of the defilements. What five? Here, monks, a monk dwells contemplating foulness in…
item.description:
“Monks, these five things, when developed and cultivated, lead to the destruction of the defilements. What five? Here, monks, a monk dwells contemplating foulness in the body, with the perception of disgust for food, with the perception of non-delight in the entire world, contemplating impermanence in all formations, and the perception of death is well established within him. These five things, monks, when developed and cultivated, lead to the destruction of the defilements.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.70
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: The Blessed One: “Seeing craving, aversion, and lust, There is no desire for sexual intercourse. What is this, so full of urine and feces? I…
item.description:
The Blessed One:
“Seeing craving, aversion, and lust,
There is no desire for sexual intercourse.
What is this, so full of urine and feces?
I would not wish even to touch it with my foot.”

From: Sutta Nipāta 4.9
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen wholesome states arise and arisen unwholesome states decline, as fewness of wishes. For one…
item.description:
“I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen wholesome states arise and arisen unwholesome states decline, as fewness of wishes. For one with few wishes, monks, unarisen wholesome states arise and arisen unwholesome states decline.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 1.63
📖 Read this sutta
